title: Halim (Wheat & Meat Porridge)
description: A comforting, slow-cooked Persian porridge made of wheat and meat (turkey, lamb, or beef), heavily pounded into a smooth, thick consistency, often enjoyed for breakfast.

ingredients.item: Lamb shoulder, beef shank, or turkey breast

instructions.text: Drain the overnight-soaked wheat. In a large, heavy-bottomed pot, bring 8 cups of water to a boil. Add the soaked wheat. Reduce heat to low, cover, and let it simmer gently for 3-4 hours, stirring occasionally until the wheat kernels burst and become very soft.

In a separate pot, place the meat, the whole onion, and 2 cups of water. Bring to a boil, skim off any foam, reduce heat, cover, and simmer for 2-3 hours until the meat is extremely tender and falling off the bone.
Remove the meat from its broth. Discard the onion and bones. Shred the meat very finely using two forks. Save the meat broth.

Once the wheat is completely soft and mushy, use an immersion blender to puree it partially. You want a smooth, thick paste, but some texture can remain. If it's too thick, add some of the saved meat broth.

Add the finely shredded meat and salt to the blended wheat. Now comes the most important part: stirring and mashing. Using a heavy wooden spoon or a meat masher (goosht-koob), vigorously stir and pound the mixture against the sides of the pot over low heat for 30-45 minutes. This creates the signature elastic, sticky texture of Halim.
Cook until the mixture is thick, cohesive, and stretchy. It should not be watery.
Ladle the hot Halim into wide, shallow bowls.
To serve, create a well in the center, pour in melted butter, and generously sprinkle ground cinnamon and sugar over the top according to personal taste.

tips: The secret to authentic Halim is the vigorous physical pounding (kash-dar kardane halim) which activates the gluten and shreds the meat to the point where it becomes one with the wheat. Slow cookers are great for cooking the wheat overnight.
storage_notes: Store in the fridge for up to 3 days. It will thicken significantly as it cools. Reheat gently on the stove with a splash of water or milk, stirring constantly.
serve_with: Freshly baked Barbari or Sangak bread, hot black tea.

substitutions.note: Bulgur cooks much faster but will not yield the exact same elastic texture as whole peeled wheat.
Chicken is leaner and faster to cook, but turkey, beef, or lamb provide a deeper traditional flavor.
